messages.author: Tristan [G5TGX]
messages.text: I used this recently: Hi, I'm a local amateur radio enthusiast, based just down the road. I'm currently looking into extending out the Meshcore decentralised network in the area. We don't have great coverage in/around Guildford and Godalming, and I'm hopeful this can be improved so that we can link into the wider mesh. Details on Meshcore: (LINK HERE) I'm wondering if you are already doing anything with this, and if there would be any interest in you hosting a node in your facility? As these are LoRA and 833 Mhz based, the size is pretty tiny, and they only need a 5V power supply or solar (And I'd be more than happy to provide all this). Looking forward to hearing from you.

messages.reply_html:
Bryan#1364 (CATM)
Hi Peter I can't find the post now but saw you mention restricting hops somewhere so I'll just mention it here. I know it seems sensible but it's the road meshtastic went down and it's what removes the message reliability. I saw a case here the other day where Andy who has a direct strong SNR signal to me sent a message that arrived via 18 hops. This is rare as it's normally a single hop but if that hop happens to clash and get dropped then then that packet will get delivered to all those repeaters anyway but if I restrict my repeater to 15 hops that message would be lost. It is perfectly possible for any London message to use up 10 or more hops before hitting any of the big hilltop repeaters around London so by restricting hops on your local repeater you are reducing the reliability that meshcore gives.
